item.title: Re: Provisional Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
No problem ;)
I sometimes find it fun to look for the original sources of the images. It almost somewhat feels like a treasure hunt.

item.title: Re: Provisional Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
Ahh, thank you so much! :D
As it turned out this image is a work of the US federal government, I think "site_entrance.jpg" will be considered as public domain as well.

item.title: Re: Provisional Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if this helps but here ya go.

Possible source of "site_entrance.jpg"

item.title: Provisional Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
Note: Image source of "site_entrance.jpg" is still unknown.

  • neutron_star.png
  • supernova_w49b.png

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public domain

Title: File:CassiopeiaNASAPIA03519.jpg
Author: NASA/JPL-Caltech/STScI/CXC/SAO Animation: NASA/JPL-Caltech/Univ. of Ariz./STScI/CXC/SAO
Uploaded on: 2011

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public domain

Title: File:Supernova Remnant W49B in x-ray, radio, and infrared.jpg
Author: X-ray: NASA/CXC/MIT/L.Lopez et al.; Infrared: Palomar; Radio: NSF/NRAO/VLA
Uploaded on: 2014

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wonderful. +1

Just like others above, my main concern is the destruction generated even after the aliens managed to turn off the machine and learned someone was tinkering. I don’t understand why the neutrino beam would be kept on, or why the facility would be completely unsecured in the first place.

In fact, I don’t get why the O5-council would decide to stop a ray that’s being going on for a billion years, specially as an urgent priority.

Also, I don’t understand the whole FTL dilemma. There is much worse within the Foundation (even at the time this was written) regarding physics-breaking, so why would it be necessary to communicate with the MIT at all? An attempt to make sense of the physics behind this, and the connection with mainstream institutions both seem out-of-place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel bad for saying it, but the aliens really should've known better than to leave it at a single-point-of-failure scenario. If they had over a billion years and they were clearly still actively monitoring the station surely they could have created redundant systems that would prevent this sort of situation from blowing them all to subatomic detritus.

Sure, you probably wouldn't expect that intelligent life would crop up and start messing with your stuff, but do you really want to stare quadrillions of lives on that?

(By the by, my head canon is that there were plenty of survivors of those races outside of those galaxies and they didn't destroy us for precisely that reason - they blame themselves for allowing such a situation to arise in the first place. You can't really blame the ants for chewing through the wires when they don't know any better.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this was pretty great. I'd love to know the full story behind why this was created.

item.title: Re: Title
item.content:encoded: The neutrino stream is the key - it is essentially the trigger for a galaxy-sized superweapon that the Foundation accidentally pulled. Crucially, the stream is a line that was interrupted to start the weapon… much like how a tripwire must be tripped to activate a trap.

item.title: Title
item.content:encoded: Just a question-why is the title tripwire? The SCP is somewhat interesting, but the title doesn't make much sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ahh this scp
alias :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Secure, Contain, Accidentally break the galaxy, use SCP-2000 and other Thaumiels to fix it Foundation. Or SCABTGUSCP-2000AOTTFI Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Secure, Contain, Accidentally Break the Galaxy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If tachyons exist and interact with normal matter in a reliable and detectable way (as they would if they produced Cherekov radiation) then we would be able to detect them, and for example would be able to communicate with the past and receive messages from the future. In Benford"s Timescape a specialized nMRI is needed to do this, not just a pool of water and I trust Benford"s physics, especially his particle physics.

Therefore either tachyons don't exist, or they don't interact (or weakly interact).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On Hercules Rockefeller's comment above:

It's like if humans built some important device on an island, that wouldn't mean the island itself is important, or that the wildlife there is special.

Agreed. It actually might imply exactly the opposite- you'd put something on an island in order to keep it away from anything else important.

On this one:

So these aliens are smart enough to build a transgalactic tachyon beam that will last over a billion years, but not smart enough to realize mutually assured destruction is a bad idea? I don't even feel bad for them then.

This assumes it was a bomb. It might have been an anchor/stabilizer placed on a habitable planet in a relatively stable solar system near the edge of the galaxy, where it was totally safe for over a billion years… until the Foundation used an anomaly to bend the laws of physics and destablize somebody's portal network/not-quite-free-energy distribution network/ansible-based pirate file sharing network…

Also, we don't know what a beam of tachyons would look like. We think they would emit Cherekov radiation, but that assumes that they interact with matter and space in the way we understand the concepts. Even if they do, not all Cherekov radiation is visible- we can only see the lowest end of it's spectrum with the highest energy range of our vision, blue-purple. Cherekov radiation tends more toward utraviolet light and x-ray, especially in high energy emissions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So these aliens are smart enough to build a transgalactic tachyon beam that will last over a billion years, but not smart enough to realize mutually assured destruction is a bad idea? I don't even feel bad for them then.

I only have two scientific issues about this scip:

1. Tachyons produce Cherenkov radiation. Even Tachyons with no charge would produce Cherenkov radiation when they interacted with matter, such as the air. This beam should produce a colossal bluish purple 'laser' coming out of the south pole at least until it leaves the atmosphere.

2. I'm pretty sure that neither pole is ever pointed towards the center of the galaxy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This is pretty solid in my opinion, and I liked the twist at the end - instead of the Earth blowing up, or some catastrophe ending humanity, other people in the galaxy pay the price. It doesn't affect us, but still manages to be horrifying. Nice.

I also don't understand why some people claim this is unrealistic because it doesn't show the Earth as being insignificant: it's never implied that the Earth isn't insignificant, it just happens to be the place the aliens built their device. How were they supposed to know intelligent life would evolve there a billion years later? It's like if humans built some important device on an island, that wouldn't mean the island itself is important, or that the wildlife there is special. And besides, even if the Earth was shown to be important on a galactic scale, this is sci-fi, anything goes.

In honor of this bad boy finally reaching +100, I've released the corresponding tale, which I'm quite proud of.

The entire tale is based on the final sentence of this skip:

"Requests by Site Director L███ to reclassify Event Omega-2100 as an XK-Scenario and utilize reality altering SCPs to retroactively prevent Event Omega-2100 have been denied."

Tale can be found here:

http://www.scp-wiki.net/regret

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sort of reminds me of a short story I read once by either Isaac Asimov. There was these maintenance guys who had to calibrate this beam thing. Basically they were on Mars or some such, and they had a power plant which produced immense amounts of power, and they sent the power to Earth by shooting a laser into a receiving unit. This thing had to be ultraprecise or else it would miss the receiving unit and vaporize huge chunks of the Earth. This seems a bit similar, but definitely *much* cooler. I loved reading this and the "super-genocide" this implied. Great work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It kicks my ass.
Yes, South Pole deserve to be bearing such a monster like mini neutron star. It is bloody South Pole anyway. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written. +1.

item.title: Re: Upvoted
item.content:encoded: My line of thought was that the aliens that beamed over to the base were the "Russians" that operated the the Dead Hand system. The civilization we blew up are the "Americans" the system had it's sights on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Considering how advanced the aliens were(time traveling tripwire) I would have to wonder what types of SCP objects they would have.
In all seriousness though, this brings up the new question, why would there be no failsafe on the deadman's switch? These beings built the weapon, why not make whomever triggers it pay a price?

item.title: Rounding?
item.content:encoded:
The aliens use base-e (~2. 71828) arithmetic, cool. In that case, some of the instances seem rounded in the wrong direction. For example, "approximately 2.71 billion meters of conduit" should then be "approximately 2.72 billion meters of conduit".

Is it bad that I think it would be kind of cool if the complex had 271.8 floors? That has a real "What the?" feel to it - and it's not entirely impossible that the race's sense of aesthetics would call for it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but what I don't understand is: the light from all the supernovae hit us at once, so the ones further away must have happened much earlier than the closer ones. Why didn't the aliens from the planets around non-blown-up stars see what was happening and stop the breaking of the signal before their stars exploded?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… was the beam of tachyons / neutrinos actually being transmitted AT the Earth FROM the center bulge the entire time? If the particles are approaching faster than light, I think somebody at the receiving end would still "see" them moving away.

Also of note: Even if the beam's transmissions were instantaneous, the light from the supernovae wasn't. The disruption of the beam was arguably the end result of whatever happened to those stars.

I don't think either point is relevant to actual physics, but they're important to my personal interpretation of what the whole complex does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Came across this one again while idly flicking through the site, and wow, it's still as amazing as ever. I absolutely love it. So horrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I think I get it more and I like it a lot. The last bit is too, with some good questions still left over to ponder.

Requests by Site Director L███ to reclassify Event Omega-2100 as an XK-Scenario and utilize reality altering SCPs to retroactively prevent Event Omega-2100 have been denied.

At the very least I would imagine that O5 would be hesitant to try to undo "atrocity a rank above genocide" for the sole reason that that race might retain knowledge of the event and seek retribution. We just massacred a couple planets, not sure they would be willing to forgive us even if we did manage to undo it properly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I like this article, it took reading the comments on it to really make me get it.

Don't know if that makes me stupid, or your article just hard to understand.

However one thing I would like to see is highlighting the aliens that come to shut down the machine are doing it to stop the humans from blowing everything up. That isn't quite so clear and I think an article should be judged as a standalone, rather then having to delve into the comments to properly get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oooh oooh oooh oooh oooh, what does this button do?
Oooh oooh oooh oooh oooh, what does this button do?
Oooh oooh oooh oooh oooh, what does this button DO?!

I now know what this button does! It breaks the galaxy!

Also, the article did explicitly state that this was after several years of study to determine what was most likely to happen. I agree you can never be too careful with what looks like a giant neutrino cannon firing into the goddamn heart of the galaxy, but it's also possible the researcher heading up the activation was a bit (okay, more than a bit) impetuous.

Kind of amused that the concept of a FTL beam bothered so many people on a site where the articles are about objects that often defy the laws of physics as we know them anyways.

Although, if the technicians were able to appear on Earth to try and interrupt the experimentation with the control panel, one would have to assume that their species isn't one of the ones that was retroactively terminated by the device's deactivation. Then again, I did just point out that the laws of physics are more like polite suggestions on this wiki…

item.title: Upvoted
item.content:encoded:
Kind of amused that the concept of a FTL beam bothered so many people on a site where the articles are about objects that often defy the laws of physics as we know them anyways.

Although, if the technicians were able to appear on Earth to try and interrupt the experimentation with the control panel, one would have to assume that their species isn't one of the ones that was retroactively terminated by the device's deactivation. Then again, I did just point out that the laws of physics are more like polite suggestions on this wiki…

EDIT: Annnnnnnnnnd it seems I hit the wrong button when trying to make a new post. I am become fail. Mods/whoever, feel free to delete this one, it's sorely misplaced. Sorry again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should retag this with the 2000 tag for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must say, I really, really liked this one! I caught almost everything you intended when reading, except base-e and the Dead man's hand.
But I had a distinctly different impression. After the technicians arrived, I assumed that they had traveled to and from the site, by way of the beam (enough data stream + technology-so-advanced-as-to-appear-to-be-magic= transporter technology). That's potentially enough reason for O5 to block the beam (to "contain" the rest of the Milky Way).
When stuff blew, I didn't assume bombs and weaponry, I assumed system disruption. My thought was basically, "Damn! They turned of part or all of the galactic entropy-suppression system!" Mutually Assured Destruction seems too small a goal when you fit a neutron-star-fueled system inside a planet in the galaxy's spiral arms and then keep rapid-response maintenance on stand-by for over a billion years.

That's just my impressions and headcanon, but including documents suggesting the Foundation's working hypotheses at various points in the timeline could make it more palatable to some people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Abettik, I think this is pretty great for only your second article. With that in mind, while it is pretty well written, but the article didn't really grab me. The last addenda just made me think. "Huh. Whoops". Not exactly memorable. And some of the retro-causality stuff wasn't really apparent to me until I looked at the comments, which, as some others have mentioned, might be worth looking at when this is posted to the mainlist.

Also in the realm of a potential rewrite. I like the implication ( ideally vauge, but present) that the O5 Council purposefully tripped the tripwire, knowing what it could do and was for.
That, at some point, they made a breakthrough on deciphering the language and realized that this was their one chance to destroy a huge potential threat to mankind, before the aliens realized that we had figured it out and disarmed the doomsday device. I can imagine a significant amount of controversy and division such a debate might cause in the council.

In my mind, that's a good hook. The Foundation considering galactic genocide. It's a big concept, to be sure, but as long as you leave it vague enough to make it fridge horror rather than bludgeon us over the head with it I think it can work.

And, like you pointed out, I can imagine an interesting aside where the researchers attempt to undo what they had done. Might make a good tale indeed.

Anyway, great job so far. I hope you are still planning on doing a rewrite, as I look forward to reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! The two changes have been made in accordance with SCP-2000 editing policy. [Editing is allowed until 13 December 2013. You may still make minor edits after that date.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this article. It's one of my favorite 2000 candidates so far, in fact. I like the fantastical story of this alien technology we've stumbled upon, and the addendum adds the "whoa" moment of the best SCPs.

I just have a few nitpics.

"Video was captured at 30 frames per minute, with frames approximately every 33 milliseconds."

30 frames per minute would be one every 2 seconds. Did you mean to say 30 frames a second?

"Since neutrinos pass through all solid matter, extreme measures were necessary to interrupt the beam."

Neutrinos don't pass through all solid matter. Otherwise it would be impossible to detect them. They just pass through nearly all solid matter. http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neutrino Just change it to "nearly all" and it will make scientific sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the praise. You've hit the nail right on the head there! I was explicitly going for an inadvertent "atrocity a rank above genocide" there at the end. Dr. L feels responsible, of course, for meddling with something he shouldn't have meddled with, but its more the O5's fault.

For the last sentence, my headcanon was always for O5 ignorance, as they were thinking this was a tracking signal or spy-information relay. I never even considered that they knew this would happen, but that sinister revelation is way too possible to ignore now. I will have to think on this angle when I do a final revision for mainlist.

Hmmm… maybe in my revision I will put something in about Dr. L being reprimanded and moved for trying something Foundation-illegal to undo Omega. Maybe a tale…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. You are right that there are some good possibilities here that I may try out later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! I'm glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. You've probably figured most of that out after reading through my explanation comments. You are right that I shouldn't need those, and that I'm doing something wrong. My problem was trying to keep just enough meat in for people to enjoy it and figure it out on their own without spelling it out for them and having it come off as boring. Obviously I was only minorly successful in this. I will rewrite it once the contest is over in an attempt at a mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. Hopefully I can rewrite this into a good mainlister after the contest ends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the article, easy upvote, I just want to make sure I understand it clearly:

Aliens, probably during some interstellar war, built a super-weapon, primed to destroy e stars, and hid the trigger on some uninhabited backwater, a.k.a. Earth. Humans evolve, the Foundation discovers the facility, investigates, experiments. Aliens do not take kindly to humans mucking around with their old war stuff, but figure we're mostly harmless, use FTL travel to get here in time, takes away the stuff we fucked with and lets us be.

O5, for some reason (headcanon: they assumed the neutrino beam was some sort of spotlight or tracking signal, so aliens would always know where earth was, and given the power we just witnessed them having, we don't want them knowing where we are), really want the beam shut off, or at least not pointed where it's supposed to be recieved. O5 authorizes a cross-test. The instant the beam is broken, the super-weapon went off, back in time, destroying e stars.

This kills all, or very nearly all, of the aliens, given that in the cosmic timescales it would take for the supernovae to reach earth, they hadn't managed to rebuild enough to undo our experiments. In other words, Site Director Lbackbox committed an atrocity a rank above genocide.

O5 refuses XK-classification, because that would require attempts to reverse it, and O5 either doesn't care about aliens, or, more sinister, knew that would happen, and wanted to destroy the aliens to prevent possible contamination of humanity/wanted more time to play with their technology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That concern honestly never occurred to me, I figured anything constantly announcing Earth's position to aliens is probably bad and they'd want to shut it off

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. There's a lot I like about this. I like the retrocausal doomsday device, and the tone of the letter is good. I like that it's getting away from "the Foundation is automatically the best at every scientific field". The science is fun. But I'm very thrown off by the O5's order to interrupt the beam. As it stands, it's completely unmotivated, and even you don't seem to know their exact reasons to a satisfactory extent. If it weren't for that, I'd upvote, but I have to remain neutral for now.

Still, I think you're underexploiting the possible motivations the O5 could have. Like, there's retrocausal stuff going on here. Couldn't the O5 have a reason based on retrocausal information?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the end was great, revealing the Dead Hand switch. That said, I'm not pleased that it took a crosstest to activate and the rest is a bit dull, all things considered. The research behind this is pretty impressive, though. I'm kind of sorry this isn't at a higher score, though I guess that's due to an earlier draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, now. Think of it from the aliens' point of view.

It's not e meters of conduit. It's meter es of conduit. :)

Still a heck of a coincidence, but not as bad of a coincidence as the aliens using the metric system. I could actually tolerate this one conceit as long as the rest of the story was self-contained and made sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Um. Okay.

It's a little underwhelming until you get to the end. Then it blows you away. Then you're just left wondering… why?

Why did the aliens let humanity live after we blew up their… um, everything?

Why did the aliens leave the bulk of the facility intact if they no longer intend to use the neutrino beam?

Why build vital galactic infrastructure on a backwater planet and then leave it completely unguarded in the first place?

Why aren't we classifying this as a Restructuring Event? That just seems cruel to me.

Why didn't the aliens just use time travel to prevent the event themselves?

Why did a galaxy-wide service interruption, presumably implying that the neutrinos were sent back in time to a point when our entire galaxy was small enough to fit within the path of the beam, possibly all the way back to the Big Bang… somehow leave the Earth intact?

And, yes, WHY did the O5 Council decide to terminate the beam? This seems like the stupidest possible thing the Foundation could do with an unknown device this powerful, and the end result was, in fact, Destroy Destroy Destroy. It's one thing to argue that the reason would be censored in-universe, but there's no reason to keep it from your audience when it's this vital to understanding the events of the story. Just use the usual clickable text gimmick. We have that for a reason. (Yes, I've seen it go all the way up to O5 before!)

Sorry to be so demanding, but this is a severely flawed execution of a good idea with a great twist ending. Just make it make sense from within the article, and you can have my +1.

Edit: If every third comment requires a Long Explanation, you are doing it wrong. Just put some of that juice into the actual article. We vote based on the article, not based on the Discussion page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just honestly find this underwhelming. I do like all the thought you put into this (and as a minor positive point, I really like the facility entrance picture you chose). But… the end result doesn't work for me. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True. Thanks for the praise.

Speaking of the time frame, you'll notice that the date of the Galaxy Blowing Up is also the release date for Star Wars: The Phantom Menace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the response. I enjoyed this skip a lot and that was the one niggling question that was bothering me. I must say I am surprised that this is in the negative. It is well written and considering the time frame of the article could be used as a in universe reason for why we don't screw with containment protocols that appear to be working correctly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. That's a fair point. I considered writing out the letter that the O5 council gave to Director Lang but I figured it would say something like "terminate the beam. We have our reasons." because it's not like we get to see into the council thought process. I also considered having a doctor write to the O5 council giving them his (wrong) theories about it being an alien listening post, but it sounded way too presumptuous in any format for him to be offering them advice. In the end I left it alone, hoping that the idea of a dense neutrino stream that aliens across the galaxy are listening to and reacting to at a moments notice would be alarming enough to warrant some kind of containment procedure beyond "leave it alone and hope they don't come back."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only quibble with this article is the question of why the 05 council felt it was a absolute priority to stop the neutrino stream. They had no idea of what the possible consequences of that action would be and if they would provoke the extraterrestrials that created it. Seems like a lot of risk for whatever reward they hoped to gain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kalinin more or less pegged my thoughts. I dunno, this just doesn't quite work for me as a whole- It slides into the "Danger zone maximus" area, if was more a…I don't know- a dead civilization from the stars base of operations still doing what it did, maybe a noticibly slower rate than first found? Just a thought- I'd upvote that happily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You don't touch things you are not supposed to, until you have fully understood them. Especially, if your job description includes handling objects that could pose threat to humanity. The foundation is mature enough to know the difference since it has been around for such a long time.

Right now a good analogy of what happened is this:

Dee Dee: What does this button do?
Seconds later, Dexter's lab is gone :).

From the show I watched as a kid: Dexter's Labratory

item.title: Re: One factual error
item.content:encoded:
Huh! What, where?

Was it in the article? I don't think so, I haven't checked the edited version

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been wondering about this complaint. How would you go about testing the control console here if you -didn't- press buttons randomly? How else do you figure it out? Even if you decide to do it, say, starting in the left upmost corner and work to the right downmost corner, that's still random because we know fuck-all about how it's supposed to work in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. I changed the random buttons in my most recent edit, and added some explanation. Give it another readthru if you have the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, I downvoted as soon as the Foundation initiated protocol "push buttons randomly." Seriously?

In terms of the actual idea, I really have no idea what's going on, so there's nothing really interesting for me to grab hold of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New version is up. Please take a look!

item.title: Re: One factual error
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

+ Long Answer
- Hide

Yes, it's impossible, it's an SCP. The signal travels Faster-than-light. Theory into Closed Timelike Curves indicates that anything faster-than-light would also have a timelike path, meaning it would allow for time travel, and in this case, a retro-active event
